news.Title: Amazon to Apply 3.5% Fuel Surcharge to Third-Party Sellers - WSJ
news.Content:
Amazon is imposing a 3.5% fuel surcharge on independent merchants that sell on its platform later this month, a move that could translate to higher prices for consumers.

The temporary fee goes into effect April 17 for many of the sellers who use Amazon’s warehouses and delivery services to store inventory and ship orders. The e-commerce giant is the latest company to apply extra fees to offset rising energy costs as the Iran war enters its second month.

Amazon said that until now it had absorbed the higher fuel and logistics costs. “But similar to other major carriers, when costs remain elevated we implement temporary surcharges to partially recover these costs,” a company spokeswoman said.

United Parcel Service and FedEx have also recently raised fuel surcharges to reflect rising oil prices. And last week, the U.S. Postal Service announced its first-ever fuel surcharge—an 8% fee that it plans to apply to packages shipped April 26 to Jan. 17.

news.Title: Rising Tesla Sales in First Quarter Didn’t Beat Wall Street Expectations - WSJ
news.Content:
Following a year marked by sluggish electric-vehicle sales, Tesla’s deliveries were up 6.3% in the first quarter compared with the same period in 2025, but that result still missed Wall Street’s expectations.

Tesla’s final quarterly tally was 358,023 EVs delivered globally. Analysts had been expecting 368,900 deliveries, according to a consensus of 19 investment firms’ estimates published by Visible Alpha.

Despite the growth from the year-ago quarter, it was Tesla’s second-worst quarter of sales since 2022. Tesla stock closed down 5.4% on Thursday. Still, the results signal a stabilization of its auto sales, after sales fell sharply last year amid backlash against Chief Executive Elon Musk’s political activism.

The company has struggled over the past two years to keep up with growing competition from Chinese automakers.

news.Title: Intel to Buy Apollo’s Stake in Joint Ireland Chip Manufacturing Facility for $14.2 Billion - WSJ
news.Content:
Intel agreed to buy Apollo Global Management’s 49% stake in the companies’ Fab 34 joint venture chip manufacturing plant in Ireland for $14.2 billion.

Intel said it would fund the purchase through a combination of cash on hand and about $6.5 billion in new debt issuances. Intel expects the transaction to add to ongoing earnings per share and strengthen the company’s credit profile beginning in 2027.

Apollo led an $11.2 billion investment for the 49% stake in 2024. The agreement was “the right structure at the right time,” Intel Chief Financial Officer David Zinsner said, adding that the company now has a stronger balanced sheet and an evolved corporate strategy.

“We appreciate Apollo’s continued collaboration to reach this outcome as we realign our capital structure with our long-term strategy,” Zinsner said.

news.Title: Microsoft Plans to Invest $5.5 Billion in Singapore by 2029 - WSJ
news.Content:
Microsoft is on track to invest $5.5 billion in cloud and artificial-intelligence infrastructure in Singapore through 2029, as demand for AI skills and computing continues to grow in the city-state.

The U.S. tech giant’s vice chair and president, Brad Smith, said Wednesday that the investment will also go toward ongoing operations.

“Our ongoing investment in cloud and AI infrastructure reflects Microsoft’s long‑term confidence in Singapore as a global digital leader,” said Smith. “We’re focused on helping people and organizations use AI by strengthening skills, increasing cybersecurity and resilience, and advancing trusted governance.”

Microsoft is also providing tools and training for tertiary students, teachers and nonprofits.

“As AI adoption accelerates, readiness remains uneven,” Microsoft said. “Many institutions and communities lack the skills, guidance, or capacity to adopt AI responsibly and effectively,” it added.

The announcement comes a day after Microsoft said it plans to invest over $1 billion in Thailand.

news.Title: Stop Breaches Before They Happen - WSJ
news.Content:
Apr 02, 2026

Stop Breaches Before They Start

How Dell and Intel are redefining cybersecurity for enterprise fleets.

By Rick Martinez, Fellow and Vice President, Office of the Chief Technology Officer, Client Solutions Group, Dell Technologies; and Todd Cramer, Senior Director, Security Ecosystem Business Development, Intel

Here’s a hard reality: Cyber breaches can linger undetected for months. That’s a significant visibility gap despite recent security advances.

Cyberattackers have adapted. As organizations shored up their defenses at the operating system (OS) level, bad actors turned increasingly to softer targets below the OS—BIOS, firmware, memory. These attacks bypass traditional software-based defenses. AI compounds the challenge: Adversaries take advantage of the same technology defenders use.

The reality is clear: The old paradigm of software-only security is no longer enough. Enterprises need hardware-rooted, multilayered defenses—and that’s exactly what Dell and Intel deliver.

Legacy devices aren’t just outdated—they’re risk multipliers.

AI is transforming productivity, but it’s also rewriting the threat playbook. Neural Processing Units (NPUs) that power AI workloads introduce new vectors, from poisoned models to data exfiltration. Attackers are leveraging AI for deepfake campaigns and hyper-realistic phishing, making identity compromise harder to detect.

Add to this the end of Windows 10 support and the emerging threat of quantum computing, and the urgency to modernize becomes undeniable. Legacy devices aren’t just outdated—they’re risk multipliers. New AI PCs require processors designed with built‑in security controls—not bolt‑on protections after deployment.

Dell and Intel have engineered a security architecture that spans above and below the operating system, activating zero-trust principles every step of the way. This layered defense strategy includes:

Dell and Intel offer the world’s most secure commercial PCs* based on third-party research. Read the full report here.

Modern threats require coordinated defense across hardware and software layers—not isolated point solutions. That’s why Dell- and Intel-based commercial AI PCs integrate seamlessly with leading software partners for unified endpoint security and optimal defense-in-depth protection.

With the Dell Trusted Device Application enabled, admins view deep, BIOS-level and firmware-level insights within third-party consoles, enriching threat detection, investigation and response. This synergy reduces dwell time and minimizes the blast radius of attacks.

Intel further enhances this model with silicon-level integrations. In fact, Intel has mapped over 30 hardware features to 150 MITRE tactics, proving the value of hardware-based countermeasures against evolving threats.

With this hardware-assisted security approach, enterprises gain a unified defense-in-depth posture that is proactive, adaptive and zero trust-ready.

Modern endpoint security requires multiple layers of defense working together.

A single breach can cripple even the most prepared enterprise. Don’t wait for outdated defenses to fail.

AI PCs are redefining enterprise security. Dell and Intel’s co-engineered solutions—working with best-in-class partner software—offer the comprehensive defense needed for modern and emerging threats.

Upgrade to Dell Pro PCs with Intel vPro®, the world’s most secure commercial AI PCs,* and transform your endpoints into cybersecurity assets.

Start your journey toward a more secure, AI-ready enterprise.

*Based on third-party analysis by Principled Technologies when comparing Dell commercial AI PCs on Intel processors versus HP and Lenovo, July 2025. Backed by Dell internal analysis of worldwide PC market, October 2025. Applicable to PCs on Intel processors. Not all features available with all PCs. Additional purchase required for some features.

This content was created and paid for by an advertiser.

news.Title: Nvidia Invests $2 Billion in Marvell as Part of Chip Partnership - WSJ
news.Content:
Nvidia invested $2 billion in semiconductor company Marvell Technology as part of a new partnership focused on specialized AI chips.

The partnership will connect Marvell to Nvidia’s AI ecosystem, offering customers building on Nvidia architectures “greater choice and flexibility in developing next-generation infrastructure,” the companies said.

The companies will collaborate on silicon photonics technology, which uses light to process data on silicon chips.

Through the partnership, Marvell will provide custom chips, while Nvidia will provide the supporting technologies and data center architecture.

“Together with Marvell, we are enabling customers to leverage Nvidia’s AI infrastructure ecosystem and scale to build specialized AI compute,” said Nvidia Chief Executive Jensen Huang.

news.Title: U.K. Competition Watchdog Homes In on Microsoft’s Business Software Ecosystem In Cloud Probe - WSJ
news.Content:
The U.K.’s competition watchdog said it would investigate Microsoft’s position in the business software ecosystem as it wields new powers under the country’s digital competition rulebook.

The Competition and Markets Authority said Tuesday that it would kick off its probe in May due to concerns about Microsoft’s position in business software, particularly with productivity software, operating systems, database management and related security services.

The Microsoft investigation comes as it and Amazon Web Services offered commitments to the regulator linked to an earlier probe on their positions in the cloud services segment. The regulator was originally checking whether the companies should receive a label known as strategic market status under the Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act. That designation gives the CMA powers to impose conduct requirements on companies linked to their business practices.

“We’re using the regime in a flexible, pragmatic way to deliver real impact, as quickly as possible, for U.K. customers,” Sarah Cardell, the CMA’s chief executive, said.

news.Title: Delta to Tap Amazon Satellite-Internet Service for In-Flight Wi-Fi - WSJ
news.Content:
Amazon.com struck a deal to provide internet access on Delta Air Lines flights, the tech giant’s highest-profile victory in the race to supply in-flight Wi-Fi.

Delta said it plans to use Amazon’s nascent Leo satellite-internet business to provide Wi-Fi on an initial 500 aircraft starting in 2028. With Leo, Delta is planning for internet speeds that will be three to five times faster than what the airline currently offers.Delta’s choice intensifies competition in the satellite-internet industry. JetBlue Airways last year announced plans to use Amazon’s service on a portion of its fleet. But Starlink, the service operated by Elon Musk’s SpaceX, has become a dominant provider for airlines, signing up customers including United Airlines, Southwest Airlines and Alaska Air Group.

Delta has looked into Starlink service, but Chief Executive Ed Bastian said he is confident that Leo’s capabilities will be at least as good or better. He said teaming up with Amazon also will allow the airline to build on its use of Amazon Web Services. The two companies plan to work together to provide more personalized content, including on seat-back screens.

Terms of the deal weren’t disclosed.

news.Title: Microsoft to Invest Over $1 Billion in Thailand on Cloud, AI Infrastructure - WSJ
news.Content:
Microsoft said it plans to invest more than $1 billion in Thailand over the next two years in cloud and artificial-intelligence infrastructure, as demand for AI computing continues to grow in the region.

The U.S. tech giant said Tuesday following a meeting between its vice chair and president, Brad Smith, and Thai Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul that the investment will build on existing partnerships to expand its data-center footprint and upskill local talent.

Microsoft also plans to invest in cybersecurity and sovereign technology in the country.

The investment aims to accelerate the adoption and use of AI across Thailand’s workforce, also known as AI diffusion, which is an indicator of productivity and long-term economic competitiveness, Microsoft said.

news.Title: These Invisible Stocks Have a Superpower - WSJ
news.Content:
Stocks are pointing to gains to start the week but oil prices are too. U.S. benchmark crude topped $100 a barrel. With the Iran conflict more than a month old, investors aren’t quite sure what to make of reports that U.S. troops could enter Iran in a risky mission to seize its uranium stockpile.

​📈 Follow our live markets data and coverage.

Have you heard of Trinity Industries, M/I Homes or Matson?

No? You’re not alone, and maybe that’s a good thing. According to Peter Lynch, one of the most successful fund managers of all time, companies that are invisible on Wall Street can be especially rewarding.

Take Trinity, which makes and leases railcars. The consistently profitable manufacturer is followed by just two analysts, only one of whom appears to have written about it recently. Apple is followed by 46, according to FactSet. Most recent media articles about Trinity are AI-generated fluff while there are thousands about Apple and its products.

That doesn’t necessarily make Trinity, Matson or M/I Homes a better buy. But the fact that all three trade at single-digit price-to-earnings ratios, about one-fourth Apple’s multiple, might have as much to do with their obscurity as their quality. And all three have beaten the S&P 500 and small-capitalization Russell 2000 this century.

news.Keywords: AI Policy, Government Contracts, Regulatory Landscape

news.Keywords: AI Chips, Manufacturing Expansion, Future Technology, Strategic Vision

news.Title: Meta Agrees to Fund Local Energy Infrastructure for Louisiana Data Center - WSJ

• The deal includes funding for natural gas plants, transmission lines, and battery storage.
• Meta also committed to new renewable resources and exploring nuclear power.
• The project is expected to bring customer savings but raises concerns about infrastructure costs.
news.Keywords: Data Center Expansion, Energy Infrastructure, Renewable Energy, Regulatory Compliance

news.Title: Exclusive | Sheryl Sandberg’s Lean In Sheds Quarter of Staff, Will Focus on Manosphere Fight - WSJ
news.AI_Summary:
• Sheryl Sandberg's Lean In nonprofit has reduced staff by a quarter.
